attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old Chinese man presents to the general medical ward for investigation of increasing tiredness and weight loss. His native language is Mandarin, but he can converse in simple broken English. He is accompanied by his daughter, who speaks both Mandarin and English fluently.

His blood tests yesterday revealed iron deficiency anaemia. The medical team recommends he undergo a colonoscopy and receive iron replacement therapy.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate way to communicate this information to the patient?
options: Ask his daughter to interpret the information
Arrange for an in-person formal interpreter
Use the medical student on the team who speaks Mandarin
Explain slowly in English and assess his comprehension
attributes.explanation:
When there is a language barrier in a healthcare setting, the most appropriate action is to use a professional interpreter. Arranging for an in-person formal interpreter is the gold standard approach when available, as it allows for optimal communication including non-verbal cues and establishes a proper therapeutic relationship. This is particularly important when discussing complex medical information and obtaining informed consent for procedures such as colonoscopy.

Asking his daughter to interpret is inappropriate for several reasons. Using family members as interpreters compromises patient confidentiality, may create emotional burden for the family member, introduces potential conflicts of interest, and risks inaccurate translation of medical terminology. The daughter may also filter information based on cultural considerations or personal judgment about what her father should know.

Using the medical student on the team who speaks Mandarin is also inappropriate. Medical students or other healthcare staff who happen to speak the patient's language lack formal interpreter training, may not be fluent in medical terminology in both languages, and face a conflict between their clinical role and interpreter role. This approach also raises concerns about confidentiality and the power dynamics between team members. Such a dynamic may result in the student not feeling comfortable to speak up if they do not understand something that the consultant was asking them to translate.

Explaining slowly in English and assessing his comprehension is insufficient for obtaining informed consent for a procedure. While the patient has some English ability, his comprehension of complex medical information would likely be limited. Patients often nod or indicate understanding when they actually don't comprehend, particularly in healthcare settings where power imbalances exist.

Using a telephone interpreter service is a reasonable alternative when in-person interpreters are unavailable. While telephone interpretation lacks visual cues and may be less effective for patients with hearing impairments or cognitive issues, it provides access to professional interpreters who are bound by confidentiality and trained in medical terminology. However, for planned discussions in a hospital setting where in-person interpreters can be scheduled, face-to-face interpretation is preferred as the standard-of-care.

Professional interpreters undergo rigorous training and certification, adhere to strict confidentiality protocols, and maintain professional boundaries. They are skilled in medical terminology and cultural nuances that affect healthcare communication. Using professional interpreters has been shown to improve patient outcomes, satisfaction, and adherence to treatment plans while reducing medical errors.
learningPoints: Professional interpreters should always be used when there is a language barrier to ensure accurate communication and informed consent.
Family members should not be used as interpreters due to concerns about confidentiality, accuracy, and potential conflicts of interest.
The mode of interpretation (in-person, video, or telephone) should be selected based on availability and clinical context.
references: Juckett G, Unger K. Appropriate use of medical interpreters. Am Fam Physician. 2014 Oct 1;90(7):476-80. PMID: 25369625. Access at: https://www.aafp.org/afp/2014/1001/p476.html#:~:text=5%2C%2015%2C%2024-,When%20using%20an%20interpreter%2C%20the%20clinician%20should%20address%20the,directly%20in%20the%20first%20person.&text=Seating%20the%20interpreter%20next%20to,the%20patient%20facilitates%20better%20communication.&text=When%20using%20an%20interpreter%2C%20the%20clinician%20should%20allow,sentence%2Dby%2Dsentence%20interpretation.
Phillips, C, 2010. Using Interpreters - a guide for GPs. Australian Family Physician Vol. 39, No. 4, Pg 188-195.
